- Python is optional (the arithmetic is powers of 2), but useful for sanity-checking the Aut(Q) formula and the inclusion-exclusion pattern on smaller exponent

Let
$$
G=\mathbb{Z}/2^{9}\mathbb{Z}\ \oplus\ \mathbb{Z}/2^{6}\mathbb{Z}\ \oplus\ \mathbb{Z}/2^{4}\mathbb{Z},
\qquad
Q=\mathbb{Z}/2^{5}\mathbb{Z}\ \oplus\ \mathbb{Z}/2^{2}\mathbb{Z}.
Let $g\in G$ be the element
g=(2^{4}\bmod 2^{9},\ 0,\ 0),
$$
so $g$ has order $2^{5}$.

Let
$$
N=\#\{\,H\le G:\ g\in H\text{ and }G/H\cong Q\,\}.
$$
Compute $N\bmod 1000$.

Solution.
Step 1 (kernel_surjection_correspondence + reduce_to_quotient_by_fixed_subgroup).
A subgroup $H\le G$ satisfies $G/H\cong Q$ iff there exists a surjection $f:G\to Q$ with $\ker f=H$.
Moreover, requiring $g\in H$ is equivalent to requiring $g\in\ker f$, i.e. $f(g)=0$. Thus such kernels are in bijection with surjections $f$ that factor through the quotient
$$
G':=G/\langle g\rangle.
Two surjections $G'\twoheadrightarrow Q$ have the same kernel iff they differ by postcomposition with an automorphism of $Q$. Hence
N=\frac{|\mathrm{Epi}(G',Q)|}{|\mathrm{Aut}(Q)|}.
$$

Step 2 (compute $G'$ in invariant factors).
In the first direct summand $\mathbb{Z}/2^{9}\mathbb{Z}$, the subgroup generated by $2^{4}$ is $2^{4}\mathbb{Z}/2^{9}\mathbb{Z}\cong \mathbb{Z}/2^{5}\mathbb{Z}$, and the quotient is
$$
(\mathbb{Z}/2^{9}\mathbb{Z})/\langle 2^{4}\rangle \cong \mathbb{Z}/2^{4}\mathbb{Z}.
The other summands are unaffected. Therefore
G' \cong \mathbb{Z}/2^{6}\mathbb{Z}\ \oplus\ \mathbb{Z}/2^{4}\mathbb{Z}\ \oplus\ \mathbb{Z}/2^{4}\mathbb{Z}.
$$

Step 3 (count_hom_sets_by_invariant_factors).
For $A=\mathbb{Z}/2^{a}\mathbb{Z}$ and $Q=\mathbb{Z}/2^{5}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2^{2}$,
$$
|\mathrm{Hom}(A,Q)|=|Q[2^{a}]|=2^{\min(a,5)+\min(a,2)}.
$$
Thus
- for $a=6$: $\min(6,5)+\min(6,2)=5+2=7$,
- for $a=4$: $\min(4,5)+\min(4,2)=4+2=6$.

So
$$
|\mathrm{Hom}(G',Q)|=2^{7}\cdot 2^{6}\cdot 2^{6}=2^{19}.
$$

Step 4 (epimorphism_count_via_maximal_subgroups).
A homomorphism $G'\to Q$ is surjective iff its image is not contained in any maximal subgroup of $Q$.
Since $Q/2Q\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$, there are exactly three maximal (index-2) subgroups of $Q$. For $Q=\mathbb{Z}/2^{5}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2^{2}$ (with $5>2$), their isomorphism types are:
- one subgroup $M_{(4,2)}\cong \mathbb{Z}/2^{4}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2^{2}$,
- two subgroups $M_{(5,1)}\cong \mathbb{Z}/2^{5}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2^{1}$.
Any two distinct maximal subgroups intersect in $2Q\cong \mathbb{Z}/2^{4}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2^{1}$, and the intersection of all three is also $2Q$.

Hence, by inclusion-exclusion,
$$
|\mathrm{Epi}(G',Q)|
=
|\mathrm{Hom}(G',Q)|
-
\Big(|\mathrm{Hom}(G',M_{(4,2)})|+2|\mathrm{Hom}(G',M_{(5,1)})|\Big)
+
2|\mathrm{Hom}(G',2Q)|.
$$

Compute the needed Hom-sizes:

1) $M_{(4,2)}$ has exponents $(4,2)$. For $a=6,4,4$,
$$
\min(a,4)+\min(a,2)=6
$$
in each case, so
$$
|\mathrm{Hom}(G',M_{(4,2)})|=2^{6+6+6}=2^{18}.
$$

2) $M_{(5,1)}$ has exponents $(5,1)$. For $a=6,4,4$,
$$
a=6:\  \min(6,5)+\min(6,1)=5+1=6,\quad
a=4:\  \min(4,5)+\min(4,1)=4+1=5,
$$
so
$$
|\mathrm{Hom}(G',M_{(5,1)})|=2^{6+5+5}=2^{16}.
$$

3) $2Q$ has exponents $(4,1)$. For $a=6,4,4$,
$$
\min(a,4)+\min(a,1)=5
$$
in each case, so
$$
|\mathrm{Hom}(G',2Q)|=2^{5+5+5}=2^{15}.
$$

Substitute:
$$
|\mathrm{Epi}(G',Q)|
=
2^{19}-\bigl(2^{18}+2\cdot 2^{16}\bigr)+2\cdot 2^{15}.
$$
Compute:
$$
2^{19}-2^{18}=2^{18},\qquad
2\cdot2^{16}=2^{17},\qquad
2\cdot2^{15}=2^{16}.
$$
So
$$
|\mathrm{Epi}(G',Q)|=2^{18}-2^{17}+2^{16}=2^{16}(4-2+1)=3\cdot 2^{16}=196{,}608.
$$

Step 5 (compute_aut_size_for_rank2_2group; final_mod_postprocess).
We compute $|\mathrm{Aut}(Q)|$ for $Q=\mathbb{Z}/2^{5}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2^{2}$.
Choose generators $e_1,e_2$ of orders $2^{5}$ and $2^{2}$. Any endomorphism is determined by
$$
f(e_1)=(\alpha,\beta),\quad \alpha\in\mathbb{Z}/2^{5},\ \beta\in\mathbb{Z}/2^{2},
$$
and
$$
f(e_2)=(2^{5-2}\gamma,\delta)=(2^{3}\gamma,\delta),\quad \gamma,\delta\in\mathbb{Z}/2^{2},
$$
because $f(e_2)$ must have order dividing $2^{2}$, forcing its first coordinate to be divisible by $2^{3}$.

Reducing mod 2, the induced map on $Q/2Q\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ is represented by
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
\bar\alpha & 0\\
\bar\beta & \bar\delta
\end{pmatrix}.
$$
This is invertible iff $\bar\alpha=\bar\delta=1$, i.e. $\alpha$ is odd and $\delta$ is odd. (For finite 2-groups, surjectivity is equivalent to surjectivity on $Q/2Q$, so this criterion is also sufficient for an automorphism.)

Count choices:
- $\alpha$ odd mod $2^{5}$: $2^{4}$ choices,
- $\delta$ odd mod $2^{2}$: $2^{1}$ choices,
- $\beta$ arbitrary mod $2^{2}$: $2^{2}$ choices,
- $\gamma$ arbitrary mod $2^{2}$: $2^{2}$ choices.

Thus
$$
|\mathrm{Aut}(Q)|=2^{4+1+2+2}=2^{9}=512.
$$

Therefore
$$
N=\frac{|\mathrm{Epi}(G',Q)|}{|\mathrm{Aut}(Q)|}=\frac{196{,}608}{512}=384,
$$
so
$$
N\bmod 1000 = 384.
$$

Uniqueness Test
1) (Reduction to $G'$ is forced.) Any $H\le G$ with $g\in H$ and $G/H\cong Q$ is the kernel of some surjection $f:G\to Q$ with $g\in\ker f$. This is equivalent to $f$ factoring uniquely through $G'=G/\langle g\rangle$.

2) (Kernel-surjection quotient is free.) For fixed $G'$ and $Q$, surjections $G'\twoheadrightarrow Q$ with the same kernel differ exactly by postcomposition with an automorphism of $Q$. Hence the subgroup count is uniquely
$$
N=\frac{|\mathrm{Epi}(G',Q)|}{|\mathrm{Aut}(Q)|}.
$$

3) (Counts are deterministic from invariant factors.) The values $|\mathrm{Hom}(G',X)|$ for $X\in\{Q,M_{(4,2)},M_{(5,1)},2Q\}$ depend only on invariant exponents, and the inclusion-exclusion over maximal subgroups of $Q$ is uniquely determined by the structure of $Q/2Q$. The automorphism count $|\mathrm{Aut}(Q)|$ is uniquely determined by the oddness criterion on $\alpha,\delta$. Therefore the resulting $N$ and $N\bmod 1000$ are unique.

384
